title: Charmed Life — 4 (Fragment 5)
sourceText:
Cat and Gwendolen opened books. Gwendolen’s face was red, then white. As Mr. Saunders bent over Roger, her inkwell sailed up out of the socket in her desk and emptied itself over the back of Mr. Saunders’ billowing tweed jacket. Cat bit his lip in order not to laugh. Julia watched with calm interest. Mr. Saunders did not seem to notice. The inkwell returned quietly to its socket.

“Gwendolen,” said Mr. Saunders without turning around. “Get the ink jar and funnel out of the bottom of the cupboard and refill that inkwell. And fill it properly, please.”

Gwendolen got up, jauntily and defiantly, found the big flask and funnel, and started to fill her inkwell. Ten minutes later, she was still pouring away. Her face was puzzled at first, then red, then white with fury again. She tried to put the flask down, and found she could not. She tried whispering a spell.

Mr. Saunders turned and looked at her.

“You’re being perfectly horrible!” said Gwendolen. “Besides, I’m allowed to do witchcraft when you’re here.”

“No one is allowed to pour ink over their tutor,” Mr. Saunders said cheerfully. “And I’d already told you that you’ve given up witchcraft for the time being. Keep on pouring till I tell you to stop.”

Gwendolen poured ink for the next half hour, and got angrier every minute of it.

Cat was impressed. He suspected that Mr. Saunders was rather a powerful magician. Certainly, when he next looked at Mr. Saunders, there was no sign of any ink on his back. Cat looked at Mr. Saunders fairly often, to see whether it was safe to change his pen from his right to his left hand. He had been punished so often for writing left-handed that he was good at keeping an eye on his teachers. When Mr. Saunders turned his way, Cat used his right hand. It was slow and reluctant. But as soon as Mr. Saunders turned away again, Cat changed his pen over and got on like a house on fire. The main trouble was that, in order not to smudge the ink with his left hand, he had to hold the paper sideways. But he was pretty deft at flicking his book straight again whenever Mr. Saunders seemed likely to look at him.

When the half hour was over, Mr. Saunders, without turning around, told Gwendolen to stop pouring ink and do sums. Then, still without turning around, he said to Cat, “Eric, what are you doing?”

“An essay on King Canute,” Cat said innocently.

Then Mr. Saunders did turn around, but by that time the paper was straight and the pen in Cat’s right hand. “Which hand were you writing with?” he said. Cat was used to this. He held up his right hand with the pen in it. “It looked like both hands to me,” Mr. Saunders said, and he came over and looked at the page Cat had written. “It was both.”

“It doesn’t show,” Cat said miserably.

“Not much,” Mr. Saunders agreed. “Does it amuse you to write with alternate hands, or something?”

“No,” Cat confessed. “But I’m left-handed.”

words.definition: Swelling out or puffing up, typically as a result of wind.
words.example: Mr. Saunders’ billowing tweed jacket.
words.exampleText: Mr. Saunders’ billowing tweed jacket.

words.definition: In a lively or cheerful manner.
words.example: Gwendolen got up jauntily and defiantly.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen got up jauntily and defiantly.

words.definition: In a manner that shows open resistance or bold disobedience.
words.example: Gwendolen got up jauntily and defiantly.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen got up jauntily and defiantly.

words.definition: Intense, violent anger.
words.example: Her face was white with fury again.
words.exampleText: Her face was white with fury again.

words.definition: A form of words used as a magical charm or incantation.
words.example: She tried whispering a spell.
words.exampleText: She tried whispering a spell.

words.definition: The practice of magical skills, spells, and abilities.
words.example: I'm allowed to do witchcraft when you’re here.
words.exampleText: I'm allowed to do witchcraft when you’re here.

words.definition: In a happy and optimistic way.
words.example: Mr. Saunders said cheerfully.
words.exampleText: Mr. Saunders said cheerfully.

words.definition: A person with magical powers or who performs magic tricks.
words.example: He suspected Mr. Saunders was a powerful magician.
words.exampleText: He suspected Mr. Saunders was a powerful magician.

words.definition: Feeling admiration and respect for something or someone.
words.example: Cat was impressed by Mr. Saunders.
words.exampleText: Cat was impressed by Mr. Saunders.

words.definition: To cause a blur or smear of something, such as ink or dirt.
words.example: In order not to smudge the ink with his left hand.
words.exampleText: In order not to smudge the ink with his left hand.

words.definition: Neatly skillful and quick in one's movements.
words.example: He was pretty deft at flicking his book straight.
words.exampleText: He was pretty deft at flicking his book straight.

words.definition: In a way that shows lack of guilt or intent to harm.
words.example: Cat said innocently about his essay.
words.exampleText: Cat said innocently about his essay.

words.definition: Unwilling and hesitant; disinclined.
words.example: His writing was slow and reluctant.
words.exampleText: His writing was slow and reluctant.

words.definition: Subjected to a penalty for an offence.
words.example: He had been punished for writing left-handed.
words.exampleText: He had been punished for writing left-handed.

words.definition: Happening or following in turn repeatedly.
words.example: To write with alternate hands.
words.exampleText: To write with alternate hands.

words.definition: To make a sudden quick movement.
words.example: Flicking his book straight again.
words.exampleText: Flicking his book straight again.

words.definition: A habitual combination of words that appear together more often than by chance.
words.example: Meaningful multi-word expressions and collocations.
words.exampleText: Meaningful multi-word expressions and collocations.

words.definition: To fill something again.
words.example: Refill that inkwell.
words.exampleText: Refill that inkwell.

words.definition: A hollow part or a frame into which something fits or operates.
words.example: Inkwell sailed up out of the socket.
words.exampleText: Inkwell sailed up out of the socket.

words.definition: A tube or pipe that is wide at the top and narrow at the bottom, used for guiding liquids or powders into a small opening.
words.example: Got the big flask and funnel.
words.exampleText: Got the big flask and funnel.

words.definition: Confused or perplexed.
words.example: Her face was puzzled at first.
words.exampleText: Her face was puzzled at first.

words.definition: Not showing or feeling nervousness, anger, or other strong emotions.
words.example: Julia watched with calm interest.
words.exampleText: Julia watched with calm interest.

words.definition: Curiosity or concern about something.
words.example: Julia watched with calm interest.
words.exampleText: Julia watched with calm interest.

words.definition: Continuing to pour liquid without stopping.
words.example: She was still pouring away after ten minutes.
words.exampleText: She was still pouring away after ten minutes.

words.definition: Permitted or given permission to do something.
words.example: I'm allowed to do witchcraft.
